description: Single source of truth for the Starbird harm score rubric. Imported by src/lib/harmScore.ts (app) and scripts/verify-harm-score.py (Guardian). Changing this file is the only supported way to evolve the rubric. Update both the bucket definitions and the inputs explanation when ranges change.

inputs.label: Scope
inputs.detail: How many people, communities, or places are affected. 73,000 Toys 'R' Us workers weighs more than a single store closure.

inputs.label: Permanence
inputs.detail: Recoverable vs irreversible. A pollution event that clears in a year is different from PFAS contamination. A brand-name reputation hit fades; a bankruptcy that closes every US store does not.

inputs.label: Intentionality
inputs.detail: Accidental vs strategic. Dividend recaps and sale-leasebacks are deliberate value extraction, not accidents.

inputs.label: Pattern
inputs.detail: One incident vs repeated behavior. A firm with multiple bankrupt portfolio companies scores higher than a firm with one.

inputs.label: Evidence
inputs.detail: Contemporaneous, sourced reporting beats unsourced claims. A score without a documented source is a weak score.

buckets.label: Minimal
buckets.description: A small-scale concern with limited documentation. Rarely used — most entries tracked here wouldn't be in the database at all if their harm was this low.
buckets.example: A single local lawsuit, later settled.

buckets.label: Moderate
buckets.description: Documented harm at a constrained scale. One bad incident, or a small pattern of issues affecting hundreds rather than thousands.
buckets.example: A mid-size firm with a handful of documented wage-theft complaints.

buckets.label: Significant
buckets.description: Thousands affected across multiple incidents, OR systematic behavior at a smaller scale. Most PE catch-alls and mid-tier firms land here.
buckets.example: JAB Holding (55) — quality degradation across Panera/Krispy Kreme/Peet's over ~a decade.

buckets.label: Severe
buckets.description: Tens of thousands affected, or a clear pattern of sale-leasebacks, mass layoffs, or environmental damage with sourced reporting.
buckets.example: Blackstone (60) — portfolio-wide sale-leaseback strategies; Aurelius (70) — The Body Shop bankruptcy.

buckets.label: Extreme
buckets.description: Documented mass layoffs (10,000+), portfolio-wide bankruptcies, or a well-established systematic pattern with multiple high-profile casualties.
buckets.example: Apollo Global (80), Ares (82), Cerberus (90), Advent (86), KKR (88) — Toys 'R' Us collapse, 73,000 jobs.

buckets.label: Catastrophic
buckets.description: Company-ending collapses or multiple simultaneous portfolio bankruptcies at the highest documented scale. Reserved for the worst-case outliers.
buckets.example: Leonard Green (97) — Joann (19,000+ layoffs 2025) + Container Store (Dec 2024) + Prospect Medical collapse, in the same window.
